Subject: Embodied Design

Loc: Built Space / Digital Layer

Status: Framework Active

TSA · POSITION PAPER · 2026
Conceptual Infrastructure

Technosomatic
Architecture

Toward a Theory of Fully Embodied Design

"There is no such thing as a sexual relationship."

Seminar XX  ·  Lacan
00.

Preamble

Architecture has always been a hypothesis about the human body. Every section cut, every ceiling height, every threshold is a prediction: if we build it this way, bodies will behave, feel, and relate in this way. The problem is that this hypothesis has rarely been tested.

Buildings are delivered, occupied, and eventually demolished without ever returning data to the architect. The loop is open. The generative turn in AI has accelerated the production of form without closing it.

This paper proposes a framework — Technosomatic Architecture — for closing that loop. It draws on game theory, affective neuroscience, psychoanalytic theory, and spatial science to argue that the root node of all design is the fully embodied human: a body that knows more than it can say, in a space that acts on it below the threshold of language.

"The root node is always the embodied human. AI measures — it does not replace — what the body already knows."

01.

The Missing
Root Node

In computational systems, the root node is the origin — the point from which all branches grow. In current AI-assisted architectural practice, that node is missing. Form is generated from precedent images, parametric constraints, and client briefs. None of these are the human body in space.

Generative AI produces architecturally plausible form. What it cannot produce, without measurement, is human-centered form — because it has no model of somatic response as input. It is answering a question that was never properly asked.

Root Node — Missing
Embodied human body in space. Somatic response. Interoceptive signal. What the building does to the nervous system.
↓
Current Input
Precedent images · parametric constraints · client brief · formal language
↓
Output
Architecturally plausible form — without somatic accountability

The question is: what does this space do to the body that inhabits it? Answering it requires inserting the embodied human as the root node — before generation begins, not after it ends.

02.

The Fully
Visible Board

Game theory distinguishes between games of perfect and imperfect information. In a game of perfect information — chess — every player has full access to the state of the board. NPC characters are the opposite: scripted responses to triggers. They simulate agency without somatic grounding — behavior without a body.

Humans navigating built space play with imperfect information as a default condition. The body generates continuous somatic data — interoceptive signals, affective states, physiological responses — but most of it remains below conscious access. We feel that a room is wrong before we know why. The board exists; we simply cannot see it.

"Embodiment is the fully visible board. Grounding is not a wellness practice — it is a condition of epistemological completeness."

Brené Brown's concept of grounding — full embodied presence — is the condition in which the board becomes visible to the self. When dissociated, overwhelmed, or mediated through a screen, the board is obscured. We operate as NPC characters.

The Navigation Model — AI as Full Board Visibility

Near-field · Human Body

Proprioception, peripheral vision, haptic feedback. High resolution, short range. The irreplaceable somatic signal. Cannot be substituted.

Full Board · AI / Digital Layer

Real-time patterns, aggregate flows, correlations no single body can perceive alone. AI extends the range of the instrument.

Together: a fully informed player. The map without the driver is autonomous — no body in the loop. The design decision belongs to the architect who holds both.

03.

The Unthought:
Above Language

The Unthought Known — Christopher Bollas

Bollas coined unthought known to name what the body knows before language forms around it. It is not unconscious in the repressed sense. It is simply prior to symbolization — it exists above language, not below it. It is felt in the body before it can be named in the mind.

Architecture acts directly on the unthought known. The proportion of a corridor, the quality of light at a threshold, the acoustic texture of a lobby — these register in the body before any cognitive assessment begins.

Constructed Emotion — Lisa Feldman Barrett

Barrett's theory displaces stimulus-response entirely. The brain is a prediction machine running on interoception — its ongoing model of the body's internal state — combined with contextual input. Emotion is what the brain constructs when it needs to make sense of a bodily signal.

"Space does not produce emotions directly — it provides the context within which the brain constructs them. This dismantles every deterministic claim in environmental psychology."

Spatial Neuroscience — The Body in Built Space

Place cells, grid cells, cortical mapping — the brain builds spatial architecture as a cognitive structure. Ellard, Edelstein, and ANFA document how building configurations modulate stress response, cognitive load, and social behavior. Barrett provides the coherence: these physiological differences are inputs to a predictive construction process. The building provides material for the brain's ongoing emotional act.

04.

Technosomatic
as Infrastructure

Technosomatic Architecture is the conceptual infrastructure for understanding how mediated surfaces — digital or built — affect the human body's access to its own fully visible board. It studies the conditions under which somatic knowledge is amplified, suppressed, or made available for design.

The art practice from which Technosomatic emerged treated the digital interface as a site of somatic disruption: the Technosomatic Homunculus — the cortical map of the absent Other — firing against a sensory vacuum. The Biological Glitch named the moment the body refused the substitution. The Void named the space in which desire circulated without a body to land on.

"Architecture was always Technosomatic. We are only now developing the instruments to read it."

Three Registers
Theoretical
Lacan · Žižek · Bollas · Barrett. The psychoanalytic and neuroscientific model of how subjects construct experience through surface and gap.
Methodological
Behavioral, physiological, and affective measurement of the body in built space. AI surfaces patterns no single instrument reveals alone.
Design
Spatial proposals accountable to measured somatic data. The architect decides. AI extends the board.
05.

Architectural
Intelligence

Intelligence was never artificial. It was always architectural. Every building encodes accumulated decisions about bodies, movement, hierarchy, desire, and light. What AI can do is learn to read that encoding — not generate new form, but surface the intelligence already present in built space.

Architectural Intelligence is a proposed graduate design research course for SCI-Arc that inserts the root node before generation begins. Students instrument real buildings through active partnerships, build AI measurement tools trained on their own collected data, and close the loop with spatial proposals that are — for the first time — accountable to somatic evidence.

The Measurement Stack
Behavioral
Movement · occupancy · dwell time · spatial choice. The observable outputs of somatic response. The what without the why.
Physiological
Heart rate variability · galvanic skin response · interoceptive signal. The raw material from which Barrett says emotion is constructed.
Affective
Ecological Momentary Assessment. The constructed emotion label captured in situ, not in retrospect.
Contextual
What the person was doing, expecting, remembering. In Barrett's framework this is constitutive of the emotion itself — not noise.
AI Synthesis
Pattern recognition across all four layers. The gap between what the building intended and what bodies actually experienced — made visible.
06.

Co-Creation:
Fully Embodied

The outcome is a new model for the architect-AI relationship: co-creation informed by the root node, accountable to the unthought, operating with full board visibility.

The generative AI produces form — but now with somatic measurement as upstream input, not formal precedent alone. The architect co-designs in full awareness of what the measured body has already said about the space being proposed. The loop is closed.

Root Node — Inserted
Embodied human in measured space. Behavioral + physiological + affective + contextual data. The unthought known, made visible.
↓ AI synthesizes full board
Architect — Grounded Decision Maker
Near-field somatic presence + full board AI visibility. Playing with perfect information.
↓ Co-design
Generative AI — Informed Generator
Form generated with somatic accountability upstream. Not from precedent alone — from measured human experience.
↓
Output — Fully Embodied Design
Architecture accountable to the body's unthought knowledge. The loop, finally closed.

"Fully embodied: the body's unthought knowledge, measured and returned to the architect as design intelligence. The loop finally closed."

07.

Coda:
The Position

"Technosomatic Architecture begins from the premise that the most advanced design instrument available is the human body — not as object of aesthetic consideration, but as source of somatic data that predates and exceeds language."

The digital layer, at its best, extends the range of what that instrument can read. At its worst, it substitutes for it entirely — producing what the art practice named the Void: the condition of bodies in relation without bodies in the loop.

The discipline of architecture is at an inflection point where the generative capacity of AI vastly exceeds its evidential basis. We can produce more buildings faster than ever. We cannot yet account for what they do to the bodies inside them.

Architectural Intelligence is the attempt to do that accounting — first, carefully, before the generation continues.

Natasha Bajc Co-Founder, Nexus Hestia Technologies · 2026

Theoretical References

Lisa Feldman Barrett Christopher Bollas Jacques Lacan Slavoj Žižek Brené Brown Colin Ellard Eve Edelstein Roger Ulrich ANFA O'Keefe · May-Britt & Edvard Moser
